content.problemStatement:
Chef has received a challenge for which he needs your help.

Given NNN, find a permutation PPP of numbers (1,2,…,N)(1, 2, \ldots, N)(1,2,…,N) such that:


Pi⊕Pi+1≠Pi+2 , where (1≤i≤N−2)P_i \oplus P_{i+1} \neq P_{i+2} \text{ , where } (1 \leq i \leq N-2)Pi​⊕Pi+1​=Pi+2​ , where (1≤i≤N−2). Here ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.
If multiple such permutations exist, print any. If no such permutation exists, print −1-1−1.

Note that, a permutation of length NNN is a sequence of NNN integers P=(P1,P2,…,PN)P = (P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N)P=(P1​,P2​,…,PN​) such that every integer from 111 to NNN (inclusive) appears in it exactly once. For example, (2,5,4,1,3)(2,5,4,1,3)(2,5,4,1,3) is a permutation of length 555 while (2,5,2,1,3)(2, 5, 2, 1, 3)(2,5,2,1,3) is not.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of a single integer as input, NNN - the length of the required permutation.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a new line, NNN space-separated integers, denoting a permutation satisfying the condition.

If multiple such permutations exist, print any. If no such permutation exists, print −1-1−1.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T lines follow, each containing a single integer N (3 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a line containing N space‑separated integers forming a permutation of {1,…,N} such that for every i (1 ≤ i ≤ N‑2) Pi xor Pi+1 ≠ Pi+2. If no such permutation exists, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=3 no permutation exists, so -1 is printed. For N=5 the fixed prefix 5 1 2 4 3 already uses all numbers. For N=10 we output the prefix followed by the remaining numbers 6..10; every triple satisfies Pi xor Pi+1 != Pi+2. The same construction works for N=22.
N=4 is handled by the special case permutation 1 4 2 3. For N=6 we use the general construction: prefix 5 1 2 4 3 and then 6.

solution.approach: The only impossible size is N = 3. For N = 3 every permutation fails because a xor b is always the remaining number. For N = 4 a hand‑crafted permutation works (1 4 2 3). For N ≥ 5 we can fix the first five numbers as 5 1 2 4 3. After that we simply append the remaining numbers in increasing order (6,7,…,N).  • For i ≥ 5 the triple (k, k+1, k+2) gives k xor (k+1) = 1, which can never equal k+2 (k+2 ≥ 7).  • The transition 3,6,7 also satisfies the condition because 3 xor 6 = 5 ≠ 7.  Hence the constructed sequence is always valid. The algorithm is O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory besides the output array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O( Σ N ) ≤ 2·10^5
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the output list (no additional large structures).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        if N == 3:
            out_lines.append("-1")
            continue
        if N == 4:
            out_lines.append("1 4 2 3")
            continue
        # N >= 5
        perm = [5, 1, 2, 4, 3]
        perm.extend(range(6, N + 1))
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, perm)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
